coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and flexible ammonia sensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots/polyaniline hybrid at room temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gavgani, Jaber Nasrollah
Hasani, Amirhossein
Nouri, Mohammad
Mahyari, Mojtaba
Salehi, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: Here we present an innovative flexible ammonia (NH3) sensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)/polyaniline (PANI) hybrid loading on flexible polyethylene terephthalate thin film by chemical oxidative polymerization. The sensor exhibited not only excellent response, good selectivity and fast response and recovery time at room temperature but also flexibility, low cost and wearable characteristics. The experimental results reveal that the response of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid was about 5 fold higher than that of pure PANI at 100ppm of NH3 and the attained gas-sensing performance may be attributed to the increased hole like carriers by S, N: GQDs and enhanced interactions between the hybrid sensing film and NH3 molecules via π electron networks. The NH3-sensing mechanisms of the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI gas sensor based on acid-base doping/de-doping process, carriers mobility, and swelling process are highlighted.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid
Flexible gas sensor
NH3
Conducting polymer
PANI

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)
Preparation of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid
Fabrication of sensing devices based on S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesi of S, N: GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of gas sensor fabrication process.
(a) TEM image, (b) XPS spectrum, and (c) The absorption spectrum of S, N: GQDs.
(a) Raman spectrum, (b) XRD profile, and (c) FTIR spectrum of S, N: GQDs.
FTIR spectra of pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films.
SEM image of (a) pure PANI, and (b) S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films.
Dynamic responses of the flexible (a) pure PANI, and (b) S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors to 100ppm NH3 at 25°C in 57% RH.
Response transients of flexible (a) pure PANI, (b) S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors to various concentration of NH3 at 25°C in 57% RH.
Gas response of flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors towards 1–100ppm of NH3 at 25°C in 57% RH.
Selectivity of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors to various VOCs vapors of 100ppm at 25°C in 57% RH.
Effect of flexibility of the pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors in gas response of 100ppm NH3 at 25°C in 57% RH.
Response (S (%)), response time (R 1), recovery time (R 2), studied detection range (D r), materials (M) and measured temperature (T m) of the various NH3 gas sensors.

row.entry: Commercial available conducting polymer: pyrrole black

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CuTSPc@3D-(N)GFs: Copper(II) tetrasulfophthalocyanine supported on three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene-based frameworks; SWCNTs: Single wall carbon nanotubes; MWCNT: Multi wall carbon nanotube; RGO: Reduced graphene oxide.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and flexible ammonia sensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots/polyaniline hybrid at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Engineering and Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology, P.O. Box, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Engineering and Color Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, K.N. Toosi University of Technology, P.O. Box, 16315-1355 Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Elites Advanced Research Center, P.O. Box, 16765-3454, Tehran, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we present an innovative flexible ammonia (NH3) sensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)/polyaniline (PANI) hybrid loading on flexible polyethylene terephthalate thin film by chemical oxidative polymerization. The sensor exhibited not only excellent response, good selectivity and fast response and recovery time at room temperature but also flexibility, low cost and wearable characteristics. The experimental results reveal that the response of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid was about 5 fold higher than that of pure PANI at 100ppm of NH3 and the attained gas-sensing performance may be attributed to the increased hole like carriers by S, N: GQDs and enhanced interactions between the hybrid sensing film and NH3 molecules via π electron networks. The NH3-sensing mechanisms of the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI gas sensor based on acid-base doping/de-doping process, carriers mobility, and swelling process are highlighted.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) as a toxic compound has been widely used in various industries, but it injured the skin, eyes and respiratory of human above its threshold of 25ppm in the air [1]. A range of devices is available for monitoring ammonia, the most established of which are electrochemical sensors based on electrolytic cells. An overview of the various ammonia sensing technologies under development was detailed by Timmer et al. [1]. In brief, other ammonia undefined sensing devices include those based on solid state sensors [2], spectroscopic techniques [3] and conducting polymers [4]. Electrolytic devices have been around for decades but generally suffer from lower detection limits and limited accuracy. A variety of solid state devices have shown promise with detection limits down to 1ppm, though selectivity can be an issue [1]. Spectroscopic sensors range from simple, non-selective pH indicator-based sensors to complex spectrometer-based systems capable of measuring down to 1ppb NH3 [3]. Though powerful, these systems are generally large and costly, suited to the laboratory rather than low cost sensors. Conducting polymer-based sensors have been increasingly employed over the last few years with detection limits in the ppm region being demonstrated. However, these sensors can suffer from irreversible reactions leading to a reduction in response [1]. Until now, most of the NH3 sensors included inorganic, inorganic oxide/dioxide and usually be expensive and exhibited low response as well as operated at high temperature, which brings about high power consumption, and low life time [5–13]. Therefore, fabricating the NH3 sensor which is reliable, inexpensive, and operable at room temperature is urgent and significant for human demand.

Conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole, polythiophene and their derivatives has been widely investigated as chemical sensors due to fast response, low cost and operation at room temperature. Among them, PANI is widely applied as a sensing material because of its ease synthesis, low production costs, and environmental stability as well as it has excellent sensing function to NH3 gas. Nevertheless, its application as commercially real NH3 sensor has been restricted because of its low response and low thermal stability [14]. Thus, many researchers used various materials such as carbon nanostructures, noble metals and dopants to enhance the sensing performance of PANI. However, these attempts still face additional problems in getting a high response.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as promising substitute for the heavy metal-containing semiconductors based QDs, are edge-bound graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than 100nm [15,16]. With the quantum confinement and edge effects, they indicate special advantages, such as low toxicity, high fluorescence activity, excellent electronic and optical properties, robust chemical inertness, and unique photostability, and have been used for bioimaging, electrocatalyst, and optoelectronic devices [15,17,18]. To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies of the application of GQDs in NH3 sensing. Herein, due to our interest in the synthesis of graphene based materials for various applications [19–22], especially as sensors [23–26], for the first time, we developed a flexible, sensitive, selective, rapid and facile NH3 sensor based on N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)/PANI hybrid loading on polyethy−lene terephthalate (PET) thin film as sensor substrate.

Citric acid (+99%) and thiouea were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. Aniline monomers (S 99.5%) were purchased from Fluka. Ammonium persulfate (APS, 98%) as oxidant was obtained from Merck Co. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from Sigma–Aldrich Co. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The S, N: GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal process of citric acid and thiourea [27]. Briefly 0.21g citric acid (1mmol) and 0.23g thiourea (3mmol) were dissolved in 5mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 20mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and placed at 160°C for 4h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min. The obtained solid can be easily dispersed into water. The synthesis of S, N: GQDs is schematically shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant S, N: GQDs were dispersed in 10mL of water by sonication for 1h, and a S, N: GQDs suspension (noted as A) with the concentration of 1mgmL−1 was formed. To another beaker, 1mL of aniline, 30mL hydrochloric acid (HCl) and 1.2g ammonium persulfate (APS) were added. The mixture was diluted by adding 15.5mL water and sonicated for several minutes in order to form a transparent solution (noted as B). Then solution A was poured into suspension B with vigorous stirring. The mixture was allowed to be stirred for 24h in order to make sure the reaction took place completely. The resultant black powders were filtered, and washed with water twice to remove the free acid (See Fig. 2 ). Finally, the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid powders were dried at 80°C overnight in the vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of sensing devices based on S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fabricate the sensing devices based on S, N: GQDs/PANI, the typical protocols were designed as follows: The PET film (1cm×1cm) was cleaned by sonicating in acetone and washed with ethanol and dried at 50°C. 5μL of S, N: GQDs/PANI water solution was then drop casted over the PET film. After evaporation of the solution through putting the device in the vacuum oven at 80°C for 1h, interdigitated Au electrodes with 400μm interdigit spacing, 100nm thickness and 100μm wide were deposited on a flexible PET substrate by physical vapor deposition method. Then, the flexible hybrid gas sensor was baked for 1h in a furnace (Exciton, EX1200-4L) at 80°C in a nitrogen atmosphere. For comparison, pure PANI on PET substrate were prepared using a similar procedure. The fabricated S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor is displayed in Fig. 2. The thicknesses of pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films are about 6–10μm. The number of manufactured gas sensors with S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid is 6 and the measured resistance with 4-point probe technique at 10nA applied current are 6, 5.7, 6.6, 7.1, 6.34, 5.9 and 6.7kΩ with resistance variance of 0.2727. On the other hand, the measured resistance for pure PANI sensing film was averaged out 28.8kΩ.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on LEO 912AB, and the accelerating voltage was 200kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted using an S-4160 electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker (Germany) VERTEX 70 spectrometer over a range from 400 to 4000cm−1. Raman scattering was performed on a Nanofinder 30 (Tokyo Instruments Co., Osaka, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a VG multilab 2000 spectrometer (ThermoVG scientific) in an ultra-high vacuum. The elemental analysis was done by a conventional combustion method based on the burn off of the sample, and the gases are detected by a thermoconductivity detector (TCD). UV–vis (UV–vis) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2100 spectrophotometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected on an XD-3A diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation by using silicon as a standard material. The sensor resistances were measured in a closed steel chamber (lab-made) at a constant applied voltage (10V) with a LCR meter (Pintek-LCR900). The response of the sensors were then assessed towards NH3 with gas concentrations ranging from 1ppm to 1000ppm at room temperature. Moreover, the selectivity of the sensors were evaluated by the standard flow-through method towards 100ppm of NH3, toluene, methanol, acetone, ethanol, chlorobenzene, and propanol at room temperature. A constant flux of humidity controlled- dry air of about 50cm3 min−1 was mixed with the target gas source at different flow rate ratios to the desired concentrations using mass flow controllers (Alicat scientific, Tucson, USA). The relative humidity (RH%) and sensor temperature were monitored by a PT100 and HIH4000, respectively. The percentage change of the gas response S (%) is defined using the following equations:
(1)
S ( % ) =
Δ R
R
× 100
and
(2)
Δ R =
R
gas
−
R
0
where R 0 and R gas are the sensor resistance in pure air and test gas, respectively. Additionally, the effect of bending to various degrees on the response of the flexible gas sensors was investigated under exposure to NH3 pulses. The bending angle was measured using a goniometer.

The TEM image and diameter distribution of the S, N: GQDs (Fig. 3 a) indicate that they are relatively uniform with diameters of 2–4nm. They are much smaller than those of the N-free GQDs synthesized hydrothermally (∼10nm) [28,29] but similar to those of N-GQDs prepared electrochemically [30,31]. The XPS spectrum of S, N: GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3b presents 5 peaks at 533, 400, 248, 227, and 164eV, which corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, S 1s and S 2p, respectively [27]. The amounts of S and N doping in the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid were determined by elemental analysis which showed about 8.62% N and 3.85% S. That demonstrates the GQDs are successfully doped with S, N from thiourea.

The as-prepared S, N: GQDs shows the green color while under irradiation by a 365nm lamp (16W), they emitted intense blue luminescence (see Fig. 1). Fig. 3c shows the absorption bands at 335nm which attributes to n-> π* transition of the CO bond, 422, 550 and 595nm due to doping with sulfur. The absorption bands at 550 and 595nm are due to the π-> π*, and n-> π* of CS and SO [27].

The Raman spectrum of the S, N: GQDs as shown in Fig. 4 a presents the disordered (D) band at 1372cm−1, corresponding to the presence of sp3 defects, and the crystalline (G) band at 1579cm−1, related to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon [27,30]. It was observed that the S, N: GQDs has an I D/I G ratio of ∼0.74. The observed ratio for S, N: GQDs is much lower than that of the original graphene film (∼1.05) [30] suggesting that the observed disordered structures may be attributes to the intercalation of S and N atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone. These results exhibit that S, N: GQDs are highly crystalline and graphitic [27,30].

Fig. 4b shows typical XRD profile for the S, N: GQDs. The S, N: GQDs exhibited a broader diffraction peak at ∼23.2, corresponding to a d-spacing of about 0.37nm according to Bragg’s law: 2dsin θ
=
nλ, where n is an integer determined by the given order, and λ is the wavelength. This d-spacing value corresponded with its of original graphene film [30].

The FT-IR spectrum of S, N: GQDs as shown in Fig. 4c, presented the broad absorption band 3000–3500cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of OH and NH. This demonstrates there are more amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S, N: GQDs, leading to the S, N: GQDs have highly hydrophilic property [27]. The bands at 1707 and 1665cm−1 are assigned to the vibrational absorption band of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. While the bands at 1577 and 1403cm−1 are from the bending vibrations of CC and CN, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at 1089, and 633cm−1 attributes to the stretching CS and CS, respectively [27].

The FTIR spectra of pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films are illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The main characteristic peaks of pure PANI are as follows: The peaks located at 1564 and 1471cm−1, which corresponded to the CN and CC stretching and vibration mode of the quinonoid and benzenoid units, could be observed; the peaks at 1297 and 1240cm−1 are assigned to the CN stretching mode of benzenoid unit; the aromatic CH in-plane band at 1107cm−1; the shoulder and peak in the range of 840–950cm−1 are ascribed to the out-of-plane vibration of CH bond in 1,2,4-disubstitude benzene; The peaks at 1042 and 800cm−1 are ascribed to the CCl group, and the CH band in 1,4-disubstituted benzene, respectively [14,32–34]. The FTIR spectrum of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid are almost identical to that of pure PANI, but all bands exhibit small red shift as compared to corresponding bands at pure PANI. Moreover, the shoulder and peak in the range of 840–950cm−1 have disappeared. Those results suggest not only the presence of π–π interactions between S, N: GQDs and PANI, but also indicate the successful information of hybridized S, N: GQDs/PANI sensing film [14,35,36].

The UV–vis spectra of pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films are shown in Fig. S1. For the pure PANI, two absorption bands at 338 and 624nm is attributed to the π–π* transition of the benzenoid ring and benzenoid-to-quinoid excitonic transition, respectively [14,37]. In case of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid, the absorption peak at 332nm exhibits blue shifts of 6nm π–π* transition compared to that of pure PANI. These results suggest the π–π electron donor-accepter intermolecular interaction between S, N: GQDs and PANI, which is consistent with reduced initial resistance of S, N: GQDs/PANI sensing films as compared to the pure PANI [14,38].

The SEM images of pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films are shown in Fig. 6 . The pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid show uniform fibrous structure belonging to a nanometer rang. It is obvious that porous structure of the sensing films result in short response time and good reversibility toward NH3, due to the fact that gas molecules diffused more easily through the porous structure and therefore its reaction with sensing films occurs easily [14,39]. In the case of S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid, observed fibrous structure of PANI remain same and all the S, N: GQDs are homogenously surrounded with PANI, suggesting that preparation process do not have deteriorating effect on the morphology of PANI.

Fig. 7 a and b shows the dynamic responses of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors towards 100ppm NH3 at 25°C in 57% RH, respectively. It indicates that the both sensors exhibit good repeatability of response towards repeated NH3-sensing cycles at room temperature. The initial resistances of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors toward various concentration of NH3 are shown in Fig. 8 a and b at 25°C in 57% RH, respectively. The resistances of both sensors increase upon exposure to NH3 and recover to the initial value upon the removal of NH3 in air. The flexible sensor based on pure PANI showed 33.72kΩ at 300ppm of NH3. Meanwhile, the exposure of the flexible device based on the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid to 300ppm of NH3 gas can lead to the resistance of 15kΩ, which recommend that the gas sensor based on S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid exhibits much better response than its of the gas sensor based on the pure PANI. The alteration in sensors resistance may be ascribed to the adsorption and desorption of NH3 molecules of the sensing films. The details of sensing mechanism for S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor will be discussed in further. Moreover, it can be observed that flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor has much lower initial resistance than flexible pure PANI one. The conductivities of flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing films measured by 4-point probe technique at 10nA applied current are 32.8Scm−1 and 95.8Scm−1, respectively. It corresponds to a significant increase of charge carrier concentration due to S, N: GQDs incorporation. Thus, S, N: GQDs plays a dominant role in the charge transport through the PANI matrix. Additionally, it can be seen that the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor shows low response to NH3 with increase of temperature (see Fig. S2). Therefore, the optimal temperature of the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor for NH3 detection was found to be room temperature (25°C). Since the interaction between S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid sensing film and VOCs gases is exothermic, the activation energy of desorption is larger than that of the adsorption of NH3 molecules on hybrid sensing film. This revealed that the decrease in response at higher temperatures is resulted from the higher desorption rate of NH3 gas [23,24]. Moreover, the effect of humidity on the NH3 detection of flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors has been assessed with exposure to 100ppm NH3 under room temperature (Fig. S3). The sensors show good stability in gas response with humidity increase, so 57% RH was used in further experiments [40].

The response and recovery times were defined as the time to reach 90% of the final equilibrium value after the detected gas was injected and removed, respectively. The response and recovery times of flexible pure PANI gas sensor to 10ppm of NH3 are 183s and 77s, respectively, while the response and recovery times of flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor are 115s and 44s, respectively (Fig. S4).

Fig. 9 exhibits the gas response of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors towards various concentrations of NH3 in the range of 1–100ppm. At 100ppm concentration, the response of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors is about 10.1% and 42%, respectively. Also the response of flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor to higher concentration of NH3 was about 9 fold higher than that of flexible pure PANI one (Fig. S5). The detection limit of NH3 for flexible pure PANI, and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors is 1ppm and 500ppb, respectively at 25°C in 57% RH. For comparison, the response of several hybrids of PANI towards NH3 has been summarized in Table 1 . It is observed that the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid shows relatively higher NH3-sensing performances than previously reported hybrids. It is well known that high selectivity of is an important factor for a practical gas sensor. Therefore, the selectivity results of the flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors to different VOC gases at room temperature are shown in Fig. 10 . The response of flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor to 100ppm of NH3, toluene, methanol, acetone, ethanol, chlorobenzene, and propanol is 42.3, 0.5, 0.45, 0.5, 0.48, 0.51, and 0.48%, respectively. The flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor exhibits a remarkably high response to NH3 but is almost insensitive to other VOC gases.

Fig. 11 exhibits the effect of bending on NH3 sensing of flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors by varying the bending angle (θ) from 0° to 80° under exposure to 100ppm NH3. It is interesting that the response of both sensors increases with the bending angle increment. At bending angle of 80°, the response of flexible pure PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors was about 2 and 3 fold higher than that of the banding angle of 0°, respectively.

The enhanced NH3 sensing properties of flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor may be attributed to the following items:
(I)
The reversible acid-base doping/de-doping process of PANI. When the sensor give exposure to NH3, not only the dedoping process occurs, but also the PANI nanoparticles can gradually change from the conductive state to insulating state, leading to an increase of the resistance [14,52].
(II)
The intrinsic sensing properties of S, N: GQDs. Generally, S, N: GQDs indicates p-type behavior under ambient conditions, owning to the electron withdrawing nature of absorbed water, and co-doped nitrogen and sulfur, as well as oxygen containing moeties which induce a hole-like carriers [26,52–54]. Therefore, the exposure of S, N: GQDs to NH3 gas as an electron donor, can lead to a decrease in the number of charge carriers, resulting in an increase in resistance.
(III)
Swelling process of the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid by the diffusion of NH3 molecules. When NH3 molecules diffuse into polymer matrix, the swelling process can cause the S, N: GQDs as conductive pathways stay apart, deteriorating the connectivity of conductive pathways, leading to significant increase of resistant of the S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor upon exposure of NH3 [14,26,36,46].

The flexible PANI and S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensors has been successfully fabricated and characterized for NH3 sensing. The S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid were synthesized by a facile, in situ chemical oxidative polymerization. The incorporation of S, N: GQDs in PANI results in considerable improvement of NH3 response (∼42% and 385% at 100ppm and 1000ppm, respectively), excellent selectivity to NH3, and rapid response and recovery times (115 and 44s, respectively) at room temperature. In addition, the response of both flexible gas sensors considerably increases as the bending angle increases. The enhancement of sensing properties for the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid can be ascribed to the synergistic effect between the S, N: GQDs and PANI. The acid-base doping/de-doping process, carriers mobility, and swelling process are possible sensing mechanism of the flexible S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid gas sensor. Detailed analysis indicates that direct charge transfer seems to be the most dominant contribution. Therefore, the S, N: GQDs/PANI hybrid offers several distinct advantages including high response, low temperature processing, and low cost, which is expected to hold great promise for real-world applications in development of future wearable electronic technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.01.086.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ammonia sensors and their applications—a review
Fundamentals of solid state ionic micro gas sensors
Measurement of atmospheric ammonia at a dairy using differential optical absorption spectroscopy in the mid-ultraviolet
Polyaniline as a new sensitive layer for gas sensors
Macroporous WO3 thin films active in NH3 sensing: role of the hosted Cr isolated centers and Pt nanoclusters
Templating synthesis of ZnO hollow nanospheres loaded with Au nanoparticles and their enhanced gas sensing properties
Gas sensing with nano-indium oxides (In2O3) prepared via continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis
Vapor–solid growth of p-Te/n-SnO2 hierarchical heterostructures and their enhanced room-temperature gas sensing properties
Nanoparticle assemblies for biological and chemical sensing
Facile synthesis of surfactant-free Au cluster/graphene hybrids for high-performance oxygen reduction reaction
Core–shell noble‐metal@ metal–organic-framework nanoparticles with highly selective sensing property
Three dimensional N-doped graphene/PtRu nanoparticle hybrids as high performance anode for direct methanol fuel cells
Carbonized nanoscale metal–organic frameworks as high performance electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
Enhanced sensitivity of ammonia sensor using graphene/polyaniline nanocomposite
Graphene quantum-dot-doped polypyrrole counter electrode for high-performance dye-sensitized solar cells
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Layered graphene/quantum dots for photovoltaic devices
Reduction of charge recombination by an amorphous titanium oxide interlayer in layered graphene/quantum dots photochemical cells
Intumescent flame retardant polyurethane/reduced graphene oxide composites with improved mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties
Nickel nanoparticles immobilized on three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene as a superb catalyst for the generation of hydrogen from the hydrolysis of ammonia borane
Gold nanoparticles supported on supramolecular ionic liquid grafted graphene: a bifunctional catalyst for the selective aerobic oxidation of alcohols
PdCo bimetallic nanoparticles supported on PPI-grafted graphene as an efficient catalyst for Sonogashira reactions

Sensor for volatile organic compounds using an interdigitated gold electrode modified with a nanocomposite made from poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) and ultra-large graphene oxide

A room temperature volatile organic compound sensor with enhanced performance, fast response and recovery based on N-doped graphene quantum dots and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) nanocomposite
A novel bioelectrochemical sensing platform based on covalently attachment of cobalt phthalocyanine to graphene oxide
Copper(II) phthalocyanine supported on three-dimensional nitrogen-doped graphene/PEDOT-PSS nanocomposite as highly selective and sensitive sensor for ammonia detection at room temperature
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Synthesis and upconversion luminescence of N-doped graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Rapid, facile microwave-solvothermal synthesis of graphene nanosheets and their polyaniline nanocomposites for energy strorage
FTIR spectroscopic and conductivity study of the thermal degradation of polyaniline films
Electrodeposition of graphene oxide doped poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) film and its electrochemical sensing of catechol and hydroquinone
Low-cost and flexible printed graphene–PEDOT: PSS gas sensor for ammonia detection
Effect of Ni2+ as a codopant on the structure, morphology, and conductivity of nanostructured polyaniline
Preparation and characterization of graphene composites with conducting polymers
Fabrication and gas sensitivity of polyaniline–titanium dioxide nanocomposite thin film
Effect of humidity on NH3 gas sensitivity of polyaniline blend films
An ammonia detector based on a conducting polymer
Self-assembly of conducting polymer nanowires at air–water interface and its application for gas sensors
Novel flexible chemical gas sensor based on poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) nanotube membrane
Gas-sensing characteristics of dielectrophoretically assembled composite film of oxygen plasma-treated SWCNTs and PEDOT/PSS polymer
Effects of O2 plasma treatment on NH3 sensing characteristics of multiwall carbon nanotube/polyaniline composite films
Effect of NH3 gas on the electrical conductivity of polyaniline blend films
Relationship between ammonia sensing properties of polyaniline nanostructures and their deposition and synthesis methods
Fabrication and ammonia gas sensing of palladium/polypyrrole nanocomposite
Fabrication of an ammonia gas sensor using inkjet-printed polyaniline nanoparticles
Role of novel microstructure of polyaniline-CSA thin film in ammonia sensing at room temperature
Influence of dopant in the synthesis, characteristics and ammonia sensing behavior of processable polyaniline
Reduced graphene oxide–polyaniline hybrid: preparation, characterization and its applications for ammonia gas sensing
Gas sensor based on p-phenylenediamine reduced graphene oxide
High sensitivity gas detection using a macroscopic three-dimensional graphene foam network

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jaber Nasrollah Gavgani received the B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees in Polymer Engineering from Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran, in 2012 and 2014, respectively. He is currently Ph.D. candidate at Amirkabir University of Technology. His research interests include soft matters, nanocomposites, polymeric solar cells, and gas sensors.

Amirhossein Hasani received his B.Sc. in Electronic Engineering from the Sadjad University Technology in 2011. He joined K.N. Toosi University of Technology, Semiconductor Devices Lab, as a M.Sc. student. His research interests are nanomaterials and their applications in sensors.

Mohammad Nouri received his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees in Electrical Engineering from Shiraz University of technology and K.N.Toosi University of technology in 2013 and 2015, respectively. His research interests are semiconductor technology and organic electronics.

Mojtaba Mahyari was born in 1986. He received his M.Sc. Chemistry (2010) and obtained PhD in organic chemistry in 2014 from the Shahid Beheshti University, Iran. His research activities are focused on the design, synthesis new nano-supports and characterization of nanostructured materials with large surface areas (Graphene, CNT, Porous Carbon, and Nanoparticles) used as heterogeneous catalysts, photocatalysts, and materials for adsorption, separation, with a multi-technique approach.

Alireza Salehi received his B.Sc., M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees from FH Kiel, Germany; University of Bremen, Germany and University of Cardiff, UK, respectively, all in electronics. He joined the Faculty of Electrical Engineering at K.N. Toosi University of Technology as assistant professor in 1996. Since 2006, he has been a professor in Nanoelectronics in the same faculty. His research and teaching interests are semiconductor devices, analysis and fabrication. His research presently focuses on gas sensors based on several semiconductors and organic materials.
